_comment: Contemporary Vedantic composition centered on the mahāvākya 'aham brahmāsmi' (Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 1.4.10 — one of the four Great Sayings of Advaita Vedānta). The piece weaves the songwriter's original Sanskrit with classical scriptural echoes: 'aham brahmāsmi' (BAU 1.4.10), the windless-lamp simile (BG 6.19), 'tejo-bindu' (the title of a minor Yoga Upaniṣad), and the standard śāntiḥ outro. The English bridge ('Mind stilled is liberation… Like a lamp in windless air…') is the songwriter's free rendering of those classical sources, not a separate Sanskrit verse. English-only sections (chorus halves) are not surfaced as lyrics_lines.
title: Aham Brahmāsmi — Contemporary setting on the Vedāntic mahāvākya

lines.explanation: Whispered opening chant of the sacred syllable. The threefold OM is the standard Upaniṣadic invocation — each utterance covers one of the three states of consciousness (waking, dream, deep sleep), with the silence after the third standing for turīya (the fourth, pure awareness — see Māṇḍūkya 12).

lines.devanagari: चित्तम् एव हि संसारः, तन्-निरुद्धं मोक्ष उच्यते ॥ शुद्ध-बोध-स्वभावोऽस्मि, निर्विकल्पोऽहम् अक्रियः ॥
lines.iast: cittam eva hi saṃsāraḥ, tan-niruddhaṃ mokṣa ucyate || śuddha-bodha-svabhāvo'smi, nirvikalpo'ham akriyaḥ ||
lines.english_literal: The mind itself indeed is saṃsāra; its restraint is called liberation. My nature is pure awareness; I am without alternative, without action.
lines.english_poetic: Mind itself is the cycle of suffering — stilling the mind is what is called liberation. My very nature is pure knowledge; I am beyond thoughts, beyond actions.
lines.explanation: Verse 1 — CONTEMPORARY SANSKRIT COMPOSITION, but the first line is almost verbatim from the Maitri Upaniṣad VI.34 ('mana eva manuṣyāṇāṃ kāraṇaṃ bandha-mokṣayoḥ — bandhāya viṣayāsaktaṃ muktyai nirviṣayaṃ smṛtam' — the mind alone is the cause of bondage and liberation for human beings) and the Yoga Sūtras (yogaś citta-vṛtti-nirodhaḥ — Yoga is the cessation of mental fluctuations). The second half names the Self with three classical Vedantic terms: 'śuddha-bodha-svabhāva' (whose nature is pure awareness), 'nirvikalpa' (without alternative — non-dual), and 'akriya' (without action — the witness, not the doer).

words.meaning: the cycle of birth and death

lines.devanagari: यद्-दृश्यं तद्-यन्-मयं सर्वं, नाना चास्ते न दृश्यते ॥
lines.iast: yad-dṛśyaṃ tad-yan-mayaṃ sarvaṃ, nānā cāste na dṛśyate ||
lines.english_literal: Whatever is seen — all that is made of That. Diversity does not exist; nothing else is seen (in truth).
lines.english_poetic: All that is seen is made of That. Diversity is an illusion — only the One exists; nothing else is truly seen.
lines.explanation: Pre-chorus — CONTEMPORARY SANSKRIT COMPOSITION echoing a famous Vedantic teaching: 'na iha nānāsti kiñcana' ('there is no diversity here whatsoever') from the Bṛhadāraṇyaka and Kaṭha Upaniṣads, and the broader Advaita position that what appears to be many is in truth the One Brahman. 'Tan-mayaṃ' (made of That) names the entire phenomenal world as nothing other than Brahman, the favourite move of Śaṅkara's commentaries.

words.meaning: made of That, consisting of That

lines.english_literal: I am Brahman! In the light of knowledge.
lines.english_poetic: I am Brahman! I am the light of knowledge!
lines.explanation: Chorus — anchored in the canonical mahāvākya 'aham brahmāsmi' from Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad I.4.10 — ONE OF THE FOUR GREAT SAYINGS (mahāvākyas) of Advaita Vedānta, one per Veda: tat tvam asi (Chāndogya, Sāmaveda), ayam ātmā brahma (Māṇḍūkya, Atharvaveda), prajñānaṃ brahma (Aitareya, Ṛgveda), and aham brahmāsmi here (BAU, Yajurveda). 'Jñāna-tejasi' (in the radiance of knowledge) is the songwriter's addition naming the realisation as luminous insight.

lines.devanagari: चित्त-सत्ता विलीनेन, देह-शून्योऽहम् अद्वयः ॥ सर्व-पापा प्रहीणोऽस्मि, प्रकटो ज्ञान-भास्करः ॥
lines.iast: citta-sattā vilīnena, deha-śūnyo'ham advayaḥ || sarva-pāpā prahīṇo'smi, prakaṭo jñāna-bhāskaraḥ ||
lines.english_literal: Through the dissolution of mind-existence, I am bodyless, non-dual. All sins I have abandoned; I shine forth — the sun of knowledge.
lines.english_poetic: When the mind dissolves, I am without body, non-dual. All sins are gone — I shine as the radiant sun of wisdom.
lines.explanation: Verse 2 — CONTEMPORARY SANSKRIT COMPOSITION. 'Sarva-pāpa-prahīṇa' (one from whom all sins have fallen away) is a phrase from the Chāndogya Upaniṣad III.14.4 in its description of the Self in the heart. 'Advaya' (non-dual) is the technical Advaita term. 'Jñāna-bhāskara' (sun of knowledge) is the same image used in the previous song — a stock metaphor for awakened insight dispelling the night of avidyā (ignorance).

words.meaning: mind-existence, the very being of mind

lines.english_literal: I am Brahman! In the light of knowledge — point of light.
lines.english_poetic: I am Brahman! I am the light of knowledge! Tejo-bindu — the point of light!
lines.explanation: Up-tempo chorus reprise. 'Tejo-bindu' (the point/drop of light) is the title of the Tejobindu Upaniṣad — one of the 108 Upaniṣads of the Muktikā canon, a minor Yoga Upaniṣad describing the meditation on a 'point of light' between the eyebrows as a path to liberation. The phrase is the artist's invocation of that tradition rather than a verbatim quotation.

words.meaning: point/drop of light (also title of a Yoga Upaniṣad)

lines.explanation: Standard Vedic / Vedantic closing invocation — the very form used at the end of the principal Upaniṣads. The threefold śāntiḥ asks for peace from the three sources of disturbance: ādhyātmika (from within oneself — body, mind), ādhibhautika (from other beings — people, animals, environment), and ādhidaivika (from cosmic forces — fate, the gods, weather). The closing OM bookends the opening of the song.
